title: Gold’s Value as an Investment
abstract: This paper presents an approach for pricing gold from investors’ perspective. The model is based on no-arbitrage principles with minimal structural assumptions. There is no need to specify investor preferences. When fitted to match 10-year real U.S. Treasury rates, the model can replicate the salient fluctuations in the time series of gold prices since 2007. The model is also able to capture key patterns of CME Comex gold futures prices. The model implies that the majority of the value of gold is due to its role as an investment asset.

author.Urban J Jermann*: Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and NBER,United States

article.1 Gold Prices and Real Treasury Rates:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Tarun Ramadorai Tarun Ramadorai Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

For investors, gold is an asset without a yield. This raises the question how they should value gold. Because gold is used as an investment asset, it is believed to be worth more than its fundamental value as jewelry or as productive input. But how much more? This paper tries to answer these questions with an approach based on no-arbitrage principles that can be tightly disciplined by empirical evidence. While inspired by quantitative general equilibrium modeling, implementing the approach is similar to applying methods for pricing fixed income instruments.

Gold is attractive as a store of value in times of low and negative real interest rates. In these times, investors looking for stores of value bid up its price. When interest rates go up again, the price of gold declines again. However, this is not the whole story. Even in times when investors are not holding gold, the prospect that they could enter the market bids up the valuation for those who use it as jewelry or a conductor. In addition, when investors are holding gold, they take into account that gold’s valuation by users who derive utility from its good looks and conductivity puts a lower floor to the price at which they can sell their gold. In other words, gold can be viewed as a substitute for real bonds and like such bonds its value moves with real interest rates. However, gold also has option value. Because of gold’s potentially long duration, this option value could be very high.

To be more specific, gold is an asset whose value moves negatively with real interest rates, just like a bond. From investors’ perspective, gold has an embedded put option because they can sell to users at some floor price. This floor depends on the state of the economy, but is higher than the investors own valuation in that state. This floor or strike price also takes into account the probability that investors will want to buy gold again in the future. In this paper, I formalize this claim. The key feature that distinguishes gold from a tradable asset, such as a stock or a bond, is that some buyers are not getting a yield or a service flow from the asset beyond the return due to the change in the price.

My approach combines two popular valuation equations: present discounted value of cash flows and rational bubbles. Each one taken separately has serious shortcomings for valuing an asset like gold. Measuring the value of gold’s utility flows is elusive. Rational bubbles have typically nonunique values and are appealed to as a residual relative to some fundamental value. Combined however, the two equations become a pricing tool that has similar properties as fixed income derivative pricing models. I implement the approach quantitatively with a one-factor term structure model for real yields. Many term structure models with useful properties have been developed and can be used to implement this approach for high levels of precision.

As a starting point, I document the empirical relation between gold prices and real U.S. Treasury yields since the late 1970s. The real price of gold and real yields are negatively correlated, but this correlation is driven by periods with low rates. When rates were high, the correlation has been weak. My model is naturally suited to display this nonlinear relation because investors only enter the gold market when rates are low enough.

More specifically, gold prices display negative sensitivity to real rates. That sensitivity is much stronger in periods with low rates. Relatedly, real rates explain a significant share of gold price fluctuations, but much more so during periods with low rates. Silver and platinum qualitatively share these properties with gold, but the explanatory power of 10-year real yields is substantially stronger for gold than for silver and platinum. This is consistent with the fact that gold has a much more prominent role as an investment asset while silver and platinum are extensively used as industrial inputs.

Basic model properties are derived for a two-state example that can be solved in closed form. For a general Markov chain specification, I show how the equilibrium can be represented as a dynamic operator and prove that under some conditions there exist a unique fixed point. This allows for a computationally efficient solution algorithm that is applicable to rich specifications.

For a quantitative analysis of the model, I use a version of the Vasicek term structure model calibrated to real U.S. Treasury yields. For the benchmark calibration, the expected value of the price of gold is more than trice the value gold would have in the absence of investor participation in the gold market. When the model is fitted to match 10-year real yields for 1980–2023, the price of gold reproduces well the salient fluctuations since about 2007. An extension of the model to include the effects of the real USD exchange rate improves the model fit. As an additional application, the model is used to study gold futures prices. The ratio of model-implied futures prices to spot prices behaves nonlinearly, and the model is able to capture key patterns of CME Comex gold futures prices.

In the literature, there are not many equilibrium models specifically designed for pricing gold.Barro and Misra (2015)andHuang and Kilic (2019)present and study empirical properties of equilibrium models with a representative agent and rare disasters. Different from these two, my model features the interaction between investors and users, and I focus on the role of interest rate movements. The model ofBarro and Misra (2015)features constant risk-free interest rates;Huang and Kilic (2019)do not consider the interaction between interest rates and gold prices.Velde and Weber (2000)study the stability and welfare properties of bimetallic monetary systems within a dynamic general equilibrium model.

Some recent empirical studies on gold prices have emphasized the link between real gold prices and real Treasury rates, in particularJohnson (2014)and Erb, Harvey, and Viskanta (2020). I show that for a more extended sample period the link between real gold prices and real rates is weaker unconditionally and that it is driven by periods with low real rates.

Asset pricing models with agents that are heterogenous in preferences and market access have been studied, for instance, byBasak and Cuoco (1998),Barro et al. (2020),Chabakauri (2015),Chan and Kogan (2002),Chien, Cole, and Lustig (2012),Gârleanu, and Panageas (2015), andEhling et al. (2018). Unlike in these papers, my model features an exogenously specified stochastic discount factor. I show in the paper how my model can be derived from a fully specified general equilibrium model. While more limited in scope than a general equilibrium model, the model presented here offers the flexibility to closely match empirical interest rate behavior.

There is a long tradition for pricing assets with stochastic discount factors, for instance, fixed-income securitiesVasicek (1977);Cox, Ingersoll, and Ross (1985)or currencies (Backus, Foresi and Telmer (1998)). My model starts from a stochastic discount factor and adds minimal economic structure to capture the interaction between different types of agents.

Bubbles have been put forth to explain differences between the prices and the fundamental values of assets, see, for instance,Blanchard and Watson (1982),Abreu and Brunnermeier (2003), andAllen, Morris, and Postlewaite (1993). In my model, gold has no fundamental value to investors except as a store of value. Gold has a fundamental value to those who use it as jewelry or productive input, and this value increases as investors buy up some of gold’s supply.

Commodity futures prices have been studied in dynamic term structure models, for instance,Schwartz (1997),Casassus and Collin-Dufresne (2005), andLe and Zhu (2013), and in structural equilbrium models, for instance,Routledge, Seppi, and Spatt (2000),Acharya, Lochstoer, and Ramadorai (2013), andBasak and Pavlova (2016). My model combines elements from both approaches: a flexible term structure model and the equilibrium interaction of two types of agents.
This section presents properties of the empirical relation between gold prices and real interest rates. I confirm the negative relation documented inJohnson (2014)and Erb, Harvey, and Viskanta (2020) for an extended sample. However, the negative relation is weaker over the longer sample periods considered here, namely, 1975–2023 and 1980–2023 for 1-year and 10-year maturities, respectively.1I document two new stylized facts. First, the explanatory power of real interest rates for gold prices is stronger at times when rates are lower. Second, gold prices are more sensitive to changes in interest rates when rates are lower. Other precious metal prices (silver and platinum) respond to yields in a similar nonlinear way, but they have substantially weaker correlations with yields.

Average monthly prices of gold for 1975.1 to 2023.12 are computed from the daily gold fixing in the London Bullion Market. The price of gold is deflated by the CPI-U. One-year and 10-year constant maturity Treasury rates are combined with inflation forecasts from the Survey of Professional Forecaster extended with additional data for the 10-year horizon from Blue Chip Economic Indicators for 1979.10 to 1991.9.2Starting with 2003.1, 10-year TIPS rates are used for the 10-year real rates.

Figure 1displays the time-series of the real price of gold (scaled by a constant for visual convenience) alongside the 1-year and 10-year real rates (10-year TIPS are used after 2003). Yields are in general lower after 2000 and gold prices higher. It can be visually detected that the negative relation is also present at higher frequencies. For instance, starting around 2019, gold and yields have sharply moved in opposite directions. Around 2012-13, gold prices are at very high levels and 10-year rates are at their lowest since the beginning of the sample period. Before and after 1985, there are periods of approximately 3 years each where gold and yields clearly move in opposite directions.

Fig. 1 Real gold prices and real Treasury yields Open in new tab Download slide

Figure 2shows the scatterplots of real gold prices against 1-year and 10-year rates, respectively. For the 10-year rates, Treasury inflation-protected securities (TIPS) are used after 2003. From this perspective, it is clear that the negative relation between the price of gold and yields is situated in the low-yield region. Linear regression lines as well as piecewise linear and 4th -degree polynomials models are included in the plots.

Fig. 2 Real gold prices against real yields. 1975.1–2023.12 and 1979.10–2023.12 for 1-year and 10-year yields, respectively. Open in new tab Download slide

To isolate high-frequency behavior, I run regressions of percentage (log) changes in the gold price against changes in yields. The regressions are run separately for changes that end in low-yield periods and high-yield periods. The cutoffs are set so as to produce samples with the lowest 33.3% or 50% of the yields, respectively.Table 1reports the slope coefficients and adjustedR-squared. Most striking are the highR-squared for the 10-year rates in the regressions based on the periods with the lowest 33.3% of yields. For the monthly changes theR-squared is .36, for annual changes theR-squared is .51. Such high numbers make it clear that long-term real rates can be considered the main driver of gold prices during the low-rate periods. During high-rate periods the correlation between changes in gold prices and yields is typically lower, for monthly changes the correlation disappears. The slope coefficients reported in the table confirm the visual impression fromFigure 2with the highest sensitivities when rates were relatively low.

To evaluate the extent to which gold’s relation with real rates is unique, I repeat the regression analyses for silver, platinum, copper, and nickel. For better comparability, I use a common sample period for all five metals, covering 1987.2 to 2023.12. This is dictated by data availability.3Figure 3displays scatterplots for the slope and adjustedR-squared for the 10-year real rate for the 50% lowest and highest yields. Slope coefficients for silver and platinum are qualitatively similar to gold: in low-rate periods prices display negative sensitivity to interest rates, when interest rates are high coefficients are close to zero. However, theR-squared in low-rate periods for silver and even more so for platinum are substantially lower than for gold. Copper and nickel are more distinct.R-squared are close to zero for monthly changes. For annual changes, slope coefficients are positive for high-rate periods. Complete tables are presented inAppendix B.

Fig. 3 Gold versus other metals. Regression slopes and adjusted R -squared of log metal price changes (CPI-adjusted) on changes in 10-year real yields conditional on yield level: gold (GO), silver (SI), platinum (PL), copper (CO), and nickel (NI). Open in new tab Download slide

Clearly, the 10-year real rate’s explanatory power for gold has been uniquely strong among all these metals, and this motivates this paper’s focus on gold as an investment asset that behaves like a long-term bond. More generally, the distances of these metals’ statistical properties from gold seem to line up with their differences in properties and uses. While silver shares its use as jewelry and investment asset with gold, it has a more diminished role as an investment assets. For instance, silver is not stockpiled by governments as a reserve asset to any significant degree. Silver is more of an industrial input, approximately 50% of the annual supply goes to industrial use (The Silver Institute 2022) compared to less than 10% for gold (World Gold Council 2023). Similarly, platinum is used for jewelry and has only limited use as an investment asset (Huang and Kilic 2019). The large majority of copper and nickel production is used industrially.

To examine the robustness of the documented nonlinear relation between precious metal prices and 10-year real yields, I have also examined daily changes focusing on the more recent 2003.1 to 2024.5 period for which 10-year TIPS have been traded.4With a large set of daily observations, I can examine interest rate sensitivity over multiple ranges of yield levels. In particular, observations for daily changes are sorted into five equal-sized groups based on the level of the real yield at the end of a change and sensitivities are computed for each group.Table 2presents slope coefficients and adjustedR-squared for gold, silver, and platinum. For gold, interest rate elasticities decline monotonically across the five yield levels, for silver and platinum the coefficients are not exactly monotonic but also display declining sensitivity. In line with monthly and annual changes over the longer sample, theR-squared for gold are substantially higher than for silver and platinum.

article.2 Model:
Time is discrete with an infinite horizon. The model features two types of buyers of gold: investors and users. From investors’ perspective, the price of gold p t satisfies the following no-arbitrage relation p t ≥ E t M t , t + 1 p t + 1 , (1) with M t , t + 1 the stochastic discount factor (SDF). Equation (1) is a valuation equation for an asset without a yield. Equation (1) holds with equality for periods when investors are holding gold; with inequality when investors are not holding gold. The inequality represents the assumption that investors cannot short gold with users. This is critical because if the equation would always hold with equality this would be the standard equation for a rational bubble. The problem with a bubble is that it should be worth zero, or an arbitrary number if one accepts the belief that in the long run the price is increasing exactly at the right rate. In this model, there is a unique value for the price because the investors’ pricing equation sometimes holds with inequality.

Investors face no restrictions on the financial contracts they trade with each other. I assume that investors are active in other financial markets, in particular, in fixed-income and derivative markets. The SDF connects the price of gold to the prices in other financial markets.

Users derive utility from their gold holdings in each period, and the marginal valuation of this utility in terms of the numeraire good is given by v t . From their perspective, the price satisfies the present-value relation p t = v t + β E t p t + 1 . (2)

Users are assumed to be risk neutral with a constant discount factor0<β<1⁠. It is assumed that users are not the marginal pricers in fixed-income and derivative markets. Knowing the process forvtandβ⁠, the price can be determined from users’ valuation alone. However, it is not clear howvtcould be measured.

Equilibrium requires that both equations hold. A key insight is that when the two equations are combined, the model can determine the users’ utility valuevtfor the times and states of nature when both buyers are in the market. Therefore, we do not need a priori knowledge of all values ofvt. Values forvtare only needed for those times and states when users are alone holding the asset. As a starting point, we can set the marginal utility when users are holding the entire supply of gold to a constantv―>0⁠. If we are interested in how much the store of value property adds to the fundamental value, thenv―is just a normalization parameter.

As a requirement for an equilibrium we assume decreasing marginal utility for gold. Specifically, we require that equilibrium values are bounded from below by the autarchy value v t ≥ v ― . (3)

In addition to the economic reason for this constraint, it also serves to eliminate possible multiple equilibriums.

To accommodate long-run growth in the real price of gold we can let users autarchy utility grow at a deterministic rateγ. With utility evolving asγtv―with timetthe price of gold inherits the same deterministic trend. The pricing equations can be rewritten for a detrended price of gold which is stationary and therefore numerically tractable. In this case, the discount factor inequation (2)becomesβγ⁠, and inequation (1)the parameterγmultipliespt+1which is then interpreted as the detrended price level.

To summarize, an equilibrium is an exogenously given process forMt,t+1⁠, parameters(β,γ,v―)⁠, and processesptandvtfor whichEquation (1),(2)and(3)hold. The processMt,t+1can be represented in many ways. I study the model with a process specified as a finite-state Markov chain. With enough states, this is a very flexible specification, and my numerical approach developed below can handle a large number of states.

As shown inAppendix A,Equations (1),(2), and(3)can be motivated with a general equilibrium consumption-based model where users and investors optimally buy gold and where the gold market clears. The literature on consumption-based asset pricing going back toMehra and Prescott (1985)has struggled specifying utility functions that have empirically relevant asset pricing implications. Despite progress, there has not been convergence to a consensus specification. The model presented here sidesteps this issue by directly specifying an SDF but it nevertheless includes explicit economic structure that captures the interaction between different types of agents.

Rearranging the pricing equations offers intuition about key model mechanisms. Based on this, the model suggests an interpretation of gold as a real bond with a put option. The investors’ pricing equation (1) can be rewritten as p t ≥ E t p t + 1 Y t ( 1 ) + c o v t ( M t , t + 1 , p t + 1 ) with Y t ( 1 ) the one-period gross real interest rate. A lower current interest rate Y t ( 1 ) and lower future interest rates raise the current price of gold, like for a zero-coupon bond. The sensitivity of the price of gold to the interest rate depends on how long the equation is expected to hold with equality. This can be viewed as gold’s duration. From a physical perspective, this duration is infinite. With the investors’ pricing equation at times holding with inequality, the effective duration is finite.

Combining the two valuation equations for a period when investors are in the gold market, the price of gold can be written as p t = E t M t , t + 1 max [ E t + 1 M t + 1 , t + 2 p t + 2 , v t + 1 + β E t + 1 p t + 2 ] . (4)

The equation shows that gold has an option-like payoff. From investors’ perspective, the ability to sell gold to users sets a floor on the price of gold, similarly to a put option. The floor pricevt+1+βEt+1pt+2depends on users’ future marginal utility and on future equilibrium prices. As suggested byEquation (4), increasing volatility in the discount factorMt+1,t+2⁠, through the convexity of the max-operator, has a positive impact on the current price of gold, just like the volatility of the underlying asset price contributes positively to the value of an option.
article.3 Two-State Example:
To illustrate model properties, I start with a simple two-state Markov chain model. This model can be solved analytically and uniqueness of the equilibrium can easily be shown.

In every periodt, one of two states of nature is realized. Log real interest rates in state 1 and 2 are such thatr1<r2. A possible equilibrium would be such that in state 1, with a low interest rate, investors are holding gold. In state 2, investors are out of the gold market.

The SDF is represented by a 2 by 2 matrix M = [ exp ⁡ ( − r 1 ) − Δ , exp ⁡ ( − r 1 ) + Δ ( π 11 1 − π 11 ) exp ⁡ ( − r 2 ) − Δ , exp ⁡ ( − r 2 ) + Δ ( π 21 1 − π 21 ) ] .

Element m i , j in M is the discount factor if at time t we are in state i and in time t + 1 in state j . The innovation in the SDF, Δ > 0 , determines the slope of the term structure. 5 In this case, the SDF has a negative innovation going into state 1 and this is the state with the low interest rate. Therefore, the price of a two-period discount bond will be higher in state 1 than in state 2. This negative covariance between the SDF and the return on the two-period bond implies a positive risk premium, and the unconditional term structure is upward sloping. The transition probability matrix is given as Π = [ π 11 1 − π 11 π 21 1 − π 21 ] .

Clearly, E t ( M t , t + 1 ) = [ Π . ∗ M ] ⋅ 1 = [ exp ⁡ ( − r 1 ) exp ⁡ ( − r 2 ) ] .

This is a property of many term structure models which conveniently allows one to directly specify a process for the short rate.

Given that r 1 < r 2 , I conjecture that investors’ pricing equation holds with equality in state 1 and with inequality in state 2. In this case, we have three unknowns ( p 1 , p 2 , v 1 ) and three equations p 1 = v 1 + β γ π 11 p 1 + β γ ( 1 − π 11 ) p 2 p 2 = v ― + β γ π 21 p 1 + β γ ( 1 − π 21 ) p 2 p 1 = π 11 m 11 γ p 1 + ( 1 − π 11 ) m 12 γ p 2 for given parameters ( β , γ , π i j , m i j , v ― ) ⁠ . It is easy to see that this linear system of equations has in general a unique solution (except for knife-edge cases, for instance, when m 11 = m 12 = β = 0 ⁠ ). Note the variables p and v are detrended by γ t ⁠ , for conciseness the notation does not acknowledge that.

For this solution to be an equilibrium, the initial conjecture that investors are not in the market in state 2 needs to be verified, that is p 2 > π 21 m 21 γ p 1 + π 22 m 22 γ p 2 .

We also need to check that users’ marginal utility in state 1 satisfies v 1 > v ― .

The existence of a solution with these properties depends on parameter values. Uniqueness of this equilibrium can be checked by ruling out other candidate equilibriums. In this two-state case, only two other equilibrium configurations are possible. Investors either are out of the gold market in both states or are in the market only in state 2. As part of the following proposition, which solves this example in closed form, I show that these alternative equilibrium configurations can be ruled out. An equilibrium where investors are always pricing gold is ruled out by assuming enough discounting (see theN−state case below for the exact condition).

Proposition 1. Assuming r 1 < r 2 and for a range of parameter values ( β , γ , Δ , r 1 , r 2 ) for which there exists an equilibrium with investors in the gold market in state 1, the price of gold in the two states is given by

p 1 = ( ( 1 − π 11 ) exp ⁡ ( − r 1 ) + π 11 Δ 1 / γ − π 11 exp ⁡ ( − r 1 ) + π 11 Δ ) p 2 , p 2 = v ― 1 − β γ [ 1 − π 21 ( 1 − ( 1 − π 11 ) exp ⁡ ( − r 1 ) + π 11 Δ 1 / γ − π 11 exp ⁡ ( − r 1 ) + π 11 Δ ) ] ,

p1>p2>p―(withp―=v―/(1−βγ)the price of gold for users in autarchy forever),r1<ln⁡(γ)<r2⁠, and this is the unique equilibrium (for a given set of parameter values).

The proof solves the equilibrium equalities algebraically and checks the inequality conditions. SeeAppendix Bfor details.

Interestingly, gold prices in both states exceed what they would be if users are in autarchy forever:p1>p2>p―⁠. In other words, the presence of investors buying and selling gold not only raises the average gold price but also raises gold prices in all states of nature. This illustrates that even though users hold the entire supply of gold in state 2 they forecast that investors will drive up the price of gold in state 1. This can also be explained by the assumption of declining marginal utility of gold. Indeed, when investors buy up gold, this reduces the supply to users and raises users’ marginal utility. Investors’ presence cannot reduce marginal utilities below autarchy values.

For marginal utility of gold to be higher in state 1,v1>v―⁠, it is shown in the proof that the interest rate is required to be smaller than the trend growth rate in that state,r1<ln⁡(γ)⁠. The two other equilibrium configurations would requirer1>ln⁡(γ)⁠, and therefore withr1<ln⁡(γ)the equilibrium computed here is unique. Note also that the exact value forr2is not relevant in equilibrium as long as it is high enough so that investors do not want to buy gold in that state.
article.4 N-State Markov Chain Model:
This section shows how an equilibrium can be defined as the fixed point of an operator. The model can be solved numerically by iterating on this operator. Extending the guess and verify approach used for the two-state example for solving the N-state case is possible, but the operator approach is more efficient for rich specifications.

The environment is defined by(β,v―,Π,M)with0<β<1a scalar,v―aN×1vector of users utility values in autarchy when they hold the entire supply, and Π andMtheN×Ntransition probability and SDF matrices. For notational compactness define the element by element multiplicationΠ.∗M≡M~⁠.6

Define the operator T mapping N × 1 vectors p of positive real numbers into themselves as T p = max ( v ― + β Π p , M ~ p ) . (5)

A fixed point of this operator, p ∗ = T p ∗ , satisfies equation (1) and can be used to compute the users’ equilibrium marginal utility values v ∗ = ( I − β Π ) p ∗ ≥ v ― (6) to satisfy the users pricing equation (2) and the decreasing marginal utility assumption, equation (3) . Vice versa, every equilibrium p is a fixed point of the operator T .

The operatorTis monotone because all elements of Π andMare positive; that is, forp1≥p2(defined asp1,i≥p2,ifor alli=1.N⁠),Tp1≥Tp2⁠.Tis not a contraction because one-period interest rates are allowed to be negative in some states, and this impliesM~j1>1for somej, withM~jdefined as a row vector ofM~⁠. The following proposition presents a sufficient condition forTto have a unique fixed point.

Proposition 2. For the space of N -dimensional vectors P ⊆ R + N and the operator T defined in equation (5) mapping elements from P into P , the K -stage operator T K is a contraction if

max ( Ξ K 1 ) < 1

with Ξ K ≡ arg ⁡ max row [ M ~ Ξ K − 1 , β Π Ξ K − 1 ] 1 starting with Ξ 0 = I , and its fixed point is the unique fixed point of T .

The proof combines standard steps with a discounting condition that is specific to the operatorT, see the appendix for details. The nonstandard discounting condition is needed to deal with the max function and to allow for some negative short-term interest rates. This discounting property is embedded in the condition on the matrix ΞKdefined in the proposition. Intuitively, there needs to be enough discounting in the effective discount factors that are implied by the max function. The condition rules out explosive paths and “bubble” solutions where investors are always pricing gold. This discounting condition can easily be checked numerically even for largeN, and the fixed point is computed by iterating onTstarting from some initial condition.

An alternative way to solve the model extends the guess and verify approach illustrated with the two-state example to the N -state case. Partition states into a set I for which investors are holding the asset and its complement I c where users are in autarchy holding all the gold. Solve for the candidate price and utility flow vectors ( p , v ) satisfying p = v + β Π p p j = M ~ j p for j ∈ I v j = v ― j for j ∈ I c through matrix inversion. Verify that p j > M ~ j p for j ∈ I c v j > v ― j for j ∈ I .

ForNstates, there are2Npossible partitions. Practically, for the one-factor model solved in the quantitative section, fewer partitions are required. For instance, if states are ordered by interest rates, definingIby a cutoff rate is typically sufficient. For richer specifications and with a larger number of states, iterating on the Bellman operator is more efficient because there is no need to customize the selection of partitions corresponding to candidate solutions.
article.5 Quantitative Analysis:
The model is evaluated with respect to its ability to produce the empirical properties documented in Section 1, specifically, a strong explanatory power of long-term real rates for gold and the dependence of the sensitivity of gold prices to interest rates on the level of interest rates. After calibrating the model to a one-factor term structure model and documenting some properties, a second factor is introduced, the real exchange rate. The time series of gold prices produced by the model when driven by these factors is compared to the historical series of gold prices.

5.1 Calibration

A model period is a month. The SDFMt+1is specified as in a discrete-time version of the Vasicek term structure model followingBackus, Foresi and Telmer (1998). In that model,lnMt+1=−yt(1M)−σmεt+1−atandyt+1(1M)=ρyyt(1M)+(1−ρy)y¯(1M)+σyεt+1withεt+1IID normally distributed with a mean of zero and a variance of one. This model has four free parameters(y¯(1M),σy,ρy,σm)⁠;atis used to make sure thatEtMt+1=exp⁡(−yt(1M))⁠. The first three parameters determine dynamics of the short rate. The fourth,σm⁠, is the price of risk parameter. This process is approximated by a discrete Markov chain with 100 states following the Tauchen method. As calibration targets, I choose the mean, standard deviation, and first autocorrelation of the 10-year rate. The price of risk parameter is set to match the unconditional spread between the 10-year rate and the 1-year rate.7

As shown inTable 3, the model can match these four calibration targets perfectly. Regarding the nontargeted moments of the 1-year rate, the volatility is somewhat too high compared to the data while the autocorrelation almost matches.

Table 3 Open in new tab Calibration and implied real interest rates. The data have been sampled at a monthly frequency, and rates have been divided by 12 to reflect the monthly model period. The sample covers 1979.10–2023. Parameter . . . Target . . Target . . Mean y t ( 1 M ) y ¯ ( 1 M ) .000665 E ( y t ( 1 ) ) Innovation std y t ( 1 M ) σ y .0005457 Std ( y t ( 10 ) ) AR(1) y t ( 1 M ) ρ y .9877 AR(1) ( y t ( 10 ) ) Price of risk σ m − .0687 E ( y t ( 10 ) − y t ( 1 ) ) Interest rates Data Model y ( 1 ) y ( 10 ) y ( 1 ) y ( 10 ) Mean .101 .202 .101 .202 Std .196 .158 .309 .158 AR(1) .984 .988 .988 .988

In addition to the SDF, the model has two parameters that need to be calibrated, the users’ discount factorβand the trend growth rate of the price of goldγ. I setβ=1/1.0351/12=0.9971implying a discount rate that is 1% above the average of the investor-implied 10-year rate. This parameter mostly affects the level of the gold price, with little impact on dynamics.βcannot be set to values much closer to one; otherwise, the model does not have a finite solution unless the trend growth rate of the gold priceγis set to very low values.γplays a crucial role for model properties.Barro and Misra (2015)find an average annual growth rate of 1.1% for 1836–2011 with a one-standard-deviation band of(0.1%,2.1%)⁠. In my model, with all other parameters as described, the highest growth rate before gold prices tend to infinity is somewhat aboveγ=1.0111/12⁠. Based on this, a plausible range for this parameter is(1.0011/12,1.0111/12)⁠. To more tightly identify this parameter, I consider the sensitivity of the log price changes with respect to changes in the 10-year yield. A higherγproduces higher sensitivity for all maturities. To select a benchmark value, I match the average of the sensitivities for the lowest and highest 50% yield realizations in the model to the corresponding data counterparts (as reported inTable 1). This is achieved forγ=1.00951/12=1.00079⁠.

5.2 Model properties

Figure 4illustrates key model properties. The first panel on the left shows gold prices against 10-year real yields. The price of gold is scaled by gold’s autarchy value (ie, if users were holding all gold forever). As is clear in the graph, with low rates gold prices increase strongly. Even during periods of high interest rates, gold is always worth significantly more than its autarchy value. The plot nicely illustrates the option-like value profile from investors’ perspective. The lower panel on the left displays the sensitivity of the price of gold with respect to the 10-year real yield. This can be viewed as gold’s duration, and it is strongly declining with the level of interest rates. If the 10-year real rate is at 0, gold’s duration is at about 12.5. The distribution of gold prices in the model is far from normal or lognormal. The probability density function for the logarithms of gold prices in the right upper panel displays strong positive skewness. This is endogenous to the model, as short rates which drive the model are lognormally distributed.

Fig. 4 Model properties Open in new tab Download slide

Table 4 shows some quantitative properties for the benchmark parameter values and for various alternative calibrations. For the benchmark calibration displayed in the first row, the standard deviation of the logarithmic price changes, Std(ln⁡p′/p)⁠, is 2.3%. This represents the fluctuations in gold prices that come from real interest rate movements. The unconditional standard deviation in monthly gold prices 1979.10–2023 is 4.3%. This implies that interest rate movements transmitted to gold by investors were responsible for about half of the standard deviation. As will be further documented below, during the more recent years characterized by relatively lower interest rates, the share of gold price movements coming from interest rate changes has been even larger.

The first column ofTable 4displays the expected value of gold relative to users’autarchyvaluep―⁠, which would be the value if users would hold all the gold forever. This can be interpreted as measuring the value of gold that is due to investors. For the benchmark calibration on the first row, this expected value is 3.97 times the level of the autarchy value. In other words, the fact that gold is an investment asset and not just a commodity is responsible for more than two thirds of its value. As is clear from the table, this value is quite sensitive to the choices for the parametersγandβ.

As shown in the third column ofTable 4, the model implies that 47% of the time investors are not the marginal pricers for gold. Equivalently, in these times users are the only pricers and their current marginal utility for gold is at its lower bound. This happens when interest rates are relatively high. It is difficult to imagine real-world situations in which literally all investors are out of the market for gold. One interpretation is that the price of gold at these times is not exactly aligned with an SDF that prices Treasuries, and that investors with frictionless access to gold and Treasuries should not be holding gold. Investors that are still holding gold at these times must be subject to some frictions. Extending the model to account for heterogenous investors that are subject to various frictions would have the potential to explicitly produce such outcomes, but at the cost of added complexity.

Table 5compares slope coefficients from regressing 12-month changes in log gold prices on changes in 10-year real yields. Model implied gold prices are computed for the sequence of U.S. 10-year real yields 1979.10–2023. Because the state space is discrete, I am interpolating the vector of gold prices with respect to the vector of model-implied 10-year yields. Consistent with the data, the sensitivity of model-implied gold prices to 10-year real yields is substantially lower for periods with high-yields. The calibration ofγhas targeted the average of the sensitivities for the 50% sample split. As shown inTable 5, the model’s sensitivity of gold to the 10-year rate is 11.3 for low-yield periods and 3.7 for high-yield periods. Compared to the empirical counterpart, this is slightly too high for low-yield periods and slightly too low for the high-yield periods.

The table also includes gold’s interest sensitivity for a more basic model labelled RA model (for representative agent) . In this model, there is one type of agent that is both investor and user of gold. The price of gold is given by p t = v t + E t M t , t + 1 p t + 1 .

It is assumed that there is a deterministically growing utility flowvt=vγt⁠, and the model is driven by the exogenous SDFMt,t+1⁠. As is shown inTable 5, the RA model cannot produce the strong nonlinearity in the sensitivity of gold prices to interest rates. The sensitivity when yields are high is excessively high. Nevertheless, the RA model also features a sensitivity of gold prices to interest rates that is declining in the level of interest rates. In other words, the representative agent model features some convexity, but not enough to match the data. Recalibrating the RA model with a lower trend growth rateγonly weakly reduces the slope coefficients. For instance, withγ= 1, the slope coefficients are –14.2 and –11.0 with the 50% cutoff compared to –14.8 and –11.5 in the table corresponding to the benchmark calibration. This comparison shows that the two-agent model can produce a better fit of the data through the explicit interaction between investors and users. Of course, this does not rule out the possibility that one could specify an SDF and utility flows for which this RA model would match the data well.

5.3 How well does the model explain gold prices since 1980?

The only driving factor considered so far is the investors’ SDF, which has been specified to match the term structure of real interest rates. Independently measuring variations in the users’ utility flows, which could be another factor, would seem to be a hopeless task. However, considering that gold users are to a large extent non-U.S. residents who are not using the USD as their numeraire creates a role for the real exchange rate to affect the price of gold.

5.3.1 Real exchange rates

Assume that there are foreign and domestic users of gold each valuing gold in their own numeraire, and that the price of gold satisfies commodity price parity so that p t = S t p t ∗ (7) with p t ∗ the price of gold in the foreign numeraire and S t the price of one unit of the foreign numeraire good in terms of the dollar numeraire good (ie, the real exchange rate). Given the importance of non-U.S. resident gold users, we can focus on their pricing equation p t ∗ = v t ∗ + β E t p t + 1 ∗ (8) with v t ∗ the marginal utility of gold in foreign units. 8

Investors’ pricing relation, Equation (1) , is in dollar terms. Using international price parity, Equation (7) , the investors’ price equation can be written as S t p t ∗ ≥ E t M t , t + 1 S t + 1 p t + 1 ∗ and p t ∗ ≥ E t M t , t + 1 Γ t + 1 p t + 1 ∗ (9) for Γ t + 1 ≡ S t + 1 / S t the gross growth rate of the exchange rate.

The model given byEquations (8),(9), and the foreign counter part of(3)can be solved forpt∗once a process for the real exchange is specified. The price of gold in dollar terms,pt,can then be obtained from international price parity,Equation (7).

For some intuition of how this affects the price of gold, consider a special case. Assume exchange rates changes are uncorrelated with the investors’ SDF and are IID withEt+1Γt+1=1⁠. It is easy to see that in this caseΓt+1drops out ofEquation (9). This implies that the solution for the gold price in foreign unitspt∗would be identical to the solution forptin the model without the exchange rate. The only effect of introducing the exchange rate would be that to obtain the price of gold in dollar terms we need to multiply the price of gold obtained in the model without exchange rates by the exchange rate. As a more realistic specification, I solve for a model where exchange rate changes are allowed to be correlated with the SDF. Specifically exchange rate changes are correlated with the innovations to the short rate which drives the SDF.

As the real exchange rate, I consider the Real Broad Dollar Index from the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (U.S.) retrieved from FRED (RTWEXBGS, 2006–2023, TWEXBPA 1980–2006). For the 1980–2023 sample period, monthly log exchange rate changes have a standard deviation of0.013and a correlation with the innovation in the 1-year real yields of−0.24⁠. The negative correlation effectively increases the price of risk (remember innovations in the SDF load negatively on the short rate) and, everything else equal, this leads to a lower price of gold. Beyond a first-order effect on the level of the price of gold, the price dynamics are very close to the special case with a zero correlation.

5.3.2 Gold prices, 1980–2023

Figure 5displays gold prices from the model driven by yields only and for the model driven by yields and real exchange rates. The average of each series is normalized to one. Clearly, the model driven by 10-year yields can capture quite well the price movements in the later part of the sample from about 2007 onward. This is consistent with the regression evidence inTable 1that shows a tight negative relation between yields and gold prices when yields are low, which is predominantly in the later part of the sample. For the period prior to about 2007, the model-implied gold price explains little of the actual gold price fluctuations. Incorporating the real exchange rate helps the model in 2012 but not in 2020. For the time period 1995- 2007, including the exchange clearly helps the model at medium frequencies. Overall, the mechanisms and the factors represented in this model can reproduce a significant part of the gold price fluctuations since 2007.

Fig. 5 Real gold price: Model and data Open in new tab Download slide

To measure goodness of fit more formally, we can consider overlapping 12-month changes in log prices and compute the mean of the squared errors of the model from the data (MSE).Table 6in the upper panel reports 1 minus the MSE (normalized by the mean of the squared deviations of gold price changes from the mean) for different sample periods. This way of presenting values makes them directly comparable to anR-squared from an OLS regression (which we will discuss below). For instance, the last column corresponds to 2007–2023, for whichFigure 5visually indicates a good fit. The pseudo-R-squared are .38 and .45 for the model driven by yields only and for the model driven by yields and the real exchange rate, respectively. From the perspective presented in the table, it is clear that the real exchange adds explanatory power in all four sample periods considered.

A constant is included. . 1979.10 − 2023 . 2003 − 2023 . 1979.10 − 2003 . 2007 − 2023 . 1-Normalized MSE Model with yields only .16 .21 –.08 .38 Model with yields and rer .34 .30 .22 .46 RA model, yields only .05 .17 –.26 .38 OLS-regression-adjusted R -squared 10-year yield, y ( 10 ) .18 .31 .18 .45 y ( 10 ) & rer .36 .43 .41 .56 y ( 10 ) & rer & y ( 10 ) − y ( 1 ) .36 .43 .44 .56 y ( 10 ) & rer & MOVE NA .43 NA .57 y ( 10 ) & rer & CPI .39 .43 .45 .56 y ( 10 ) & rer & CB reserves .36 .47 .42 .58 . 1979.10 − 2023 . 2003 − 2023 . 1979.10 − 2003 . 2007 − 2023 . 1-Normalized MSE Model with yields only .16 .21 –.08 .38 Model with yields and rer .34 .30 .22 .46 RA model, yields only .05 .17 –.26 .38 OLS-regression-adjusted R -squared 10-year yield, y ( 10 ) .18 .31 .18 .45 y ( 10 ) & rer .36 .43 .41 .56 y ( 10 ) & rer & y ( 10 ) − y ( 1 ) .36 .43 .44 .56 y ( 10 ) & rer & MOVE NA .43 NA .57 y ( 10 ) & rer & CPI .39 .43 .45 .56 y ( 10 ) & rer & CB reserves .36 .47 .42 .58

Why does the model-implied gold price fit the data so well from 2007 onward and apparently so poorly prior to that? The model partially explains this. Interest rates are mostly lower from 2007 onward and therefore gold prices are more sensitive to interest rates. It is therefore to be expected that a model driven only by interest rates would explain a larger share of the fluctuations in gold prices from 2007 onward.Table 6confirms this as the regressionR-squared from gold prices on yields are lower in early periods.

A minor generalization of the model provides another channel through which some changes in financial markets can rationalize the model’s improved fit in the later part of the sample. Assume investors incur a per period cost κ ≥ 0 for holding gold representing, for instance, storage costs or the inconvenience of an asset with (relatively) low liquidity. The investors’ pricing equation then changes to p t ≥ E t M t , t + 1 ( p t + 1 − κ ) .

Clearly, with a largerκ, investors are marginal less often. Forκlarge enough, they will never be marginal and the SDF will have no impact on the equilibrium gold price anymore. In this case, the model driven only by shocks to the SDF would have a price of gold with a constant growth rate. In November 2004, the first U.S. gold ETF (GLD) was launched. Assets under management and trading volumes increased quickly. ETFs made gold cheaper to hold and more liquid to investors. Their introduction can be viewed as a decline inκ, which would contribute to investors being marginal more often.

What are possible factors omitted by the model and what could we expect from them in terms of additional explanatory power? Other factors beyond yield levels and exchange rates are thought of as being important for gold demand and gold prices too. For instance, gold is often thought of as a flight-to-safety asset. Given that Treasuries are also sought after during flight-to-safety periods, interest rate movements are likely to already capture some of that demand. As an additional factor, I can consider interest rate volatility as measured by the MOVE index. This could be viewed as a second factor in a richer term structure model. Relatedly, the term spread between the 10-year and the 1-year real yields could also be considered as an additional factor. Gold is also thought of as an inflation hedge and therefore the level of inflation could proxy for that demand.

The lower panel ofTable 6shows the adjustedR-squared from regressions of gold price changes on yields, real exchange rates, and several candidate factors. Clearly, as shown in the table, none of the three additional factors considered—term spread, MOVE, or CPI—add more than very marginally to theR-squared. Based on this, extensions to the SDF that explicitly represent these factors are not likely to help the model much. This negative finding does not necessarily rule out that these factors have played a role for gold. However, in terms of systematic drivers of gold prices over the last few decades, it is interest rates and exchange rates that have been dominant.

Table 6has other noteworthy results. First, for the two versions of the main model, the pseudo-R-squared based on the MSE are mostly not that much worse than theR-squared from the linear regressions. This can be viewed as a success for the model because the regressionR-squared are based on sample-specific coefficient estimates while the model has a common calibration for all four sample periods considered. Second, one exception is the early period, 1979.10–2023, where the yields-only model has a slightly negativeR-squared, while the corresponding regressionR-squared is .18. Roughly speaking, yields were still important during that time, but the model’s interest sensitivity is off. This is consistent with the results inTable 5showing the model’s interest sensitivity for high rates to be too low (the early period typically has higher rates). Third, while this shows a weakness of the model to fully capture the changing sensitivity of gold prices to yields, the model does still better than the RA model, for which the interest sensitivity does not change nearly enough to match the data. As shown inTable 6the pseudo-R-squared from the RA model are smaller for all three sample periods considered and equal for the forth. Finally, another factor missing from the model, namely, changes in central bank gold reserves (based in IMF data) has also very little marginal explanatory power, as shown in the last line of the table.
article.6 Gold Futures:
CME has deep markets for gold futures, and there is a long history of price data. In this section, I derive model-implied futures prices and compare these to the data. The model is shown to generate fluctuations in the futures to spot ratio that broadly reproduce its empirical counterpart.

Assume investors have access to one-period futures contracts at a price F t for delivery next period t + 1. CME futures contracts are denominated in dollar terms, the model is specified in real terms. Define P t as the price of gold in dollars and P t C as the price of one unit of the consumption good in dollars so that P t = P t C p t , with p t the price of gold in real terms (consumption good units). The payoff to the long side is P t + 1 − F t , and ruling out arbitrage E t M t , t + 1 P t + 1 C ( P t + 1 − F t ) = 0.

Note, for this to be an equilibrium outcome it is assumed that there are frictions on users that limit the positions they can take, and only investors have frictionless access to futures. The no-arbitrage condition can be rewritten as F t P t Y t N ( 1 ) = E t M t , t + 1 p t + 1 p t (10) with Y t N ( 1 ) = 1 / E t M t , t + 1 P t + 1 C / P t C the one-period nominal (gross) interest rate. Remember the investor pricing equation, p t ≥ E t M t , t + 1 p t + 1 ⁠ , which holds with equality if investors are in the gold market. The futures price ratio in the model therefore satisfies F t P t Y t N ( 1 ) ≤ 1. (11)

Intuitively, this equation represents the cash-and-carry arbitrage and the frictions in the model. Investors can always buy gold spot with borrowed funds and hedge it with a short futures, and this limits the futures priceFtto be no higher than the cost of carrying gold,PtYtN(1)⁠. However, when investors are not holding gold they cannot take a short spot position. In that case, the spot price including interest can be higher than the futures price. Based on our previous analysis, when interest rates are low or negative, investors are likely to be holding gold and therefore the futures to spot (including interest) ratio equals one. With high real rates, this ratio is more likely to be smaller than one.

I compute the 1-month futures to spot ratio from CME Comex futures prices by taking the ratio of the contract with a maturity of between 1 and 2 months relative to the contract that is expiring within the month. Instead of using spot prices from the London Bullion Market or Bloomberg this has the advantage of not introducing nonsynchronous prices and of keeping the maturity difference constant at one month. The 1-month nominal rate is based on the CRSP Treasury 26-week term structure.

Figure 6displays historical futures ratios alongside model-implied counterparts. The model is fitted to match either the 1-year or the 10-year rate. Because the one-factor term structure model cannot exactly fit both rates, there are some differences between the two model-implied series. Strikingly, the model produces the broad patterns of the data. The futures ratio is bounded from above by one in the model and is often at this upper bound in the second half of the sample (where interest rate were typically lower). With this restriction, the model cannot match the upward spike in the ratio in the spring of 2020. This coincides with the COVID-19 crisis, which severely disrupted financial markets. In the second half of the sample it is also clear that the model fitted to the 1-year real rate is closer to the data than the model fitted to the 10-year rate. In particular, it tracks the data better around 2006–2007 and around 2023. To match the futures ratio it is important to match the short end of the term structure and the 1-year rate is closer to that than the 10-year rate.

Fig. 6 Futures (1-month) to spot (with interest) ratio: Model vs data Open in new tab Download slide

For comparison, consider the representative agent (RA) setting where the futures ratio satisfies F t P t Y t N ( 1 ) = 1 − v t p t , (12) that is, the futures ratio is lowered below one by the utility yield for holding gold, v t / p t ⁠ . This utility yield is equivalent to what is known as a convenience yield in the commodities literature, see, for instance, Schwartz (1997) or Routledge, Seppi, and Spatt (2000) . Figure 7 displays futures ratios from the RA model. The model is only driven by term structure movements and the utility flow v t is assumed to be deterministic. The model reproduces very little of the empirical patterns. For the second half of the sample, the model is mostly about 20 basis points below its empirical counterpart. To close this 20-bps gap, the growth rate γ could be recalibrated to a higher value. 9 As discussed earlier, to reduce the excessive responsiveness to interest rates the RA model should be calibrated to a lower (not higher) growth rate γ . Abstracting form that, a higher γ not only lowers v / p t but also stabilizes the futures ratio and thus reduces the model’s ability to produce variations in this ratio. Essentially, the RA model driven only by term structure movements cannot account in any significant way for the empirical behavior of the futures ratio. For the representative agent setting to match the variations in the futures ratio would require large transitory movements in the marginal utility v t .

Fig. 7 Futures (1-month) to spot (with interest) ratio: Representative agent model vs data Open in new tab Download slide
article.7 Conclusion:
This paper values gold from an investor perspective by recognizing that users of gold as jewelry or as a productive input put a lower floor to the price of gold. This embeds an option into gold whose strike price is implicitly derived by the users’ price forecasts. A model incorporating these ideas is presented, analytically characterized, and an efficient computational algorithm is developed. Motivated by the evidence on the relation between gold prices and real interest rates, the quantitative analysis is based on a term structure model calibrated to real U.S. interest rates. The model can go a long way toward replicating the time series of gold prices since about 2007. The model captures key patterns of CME Comex gold futures prices. The approach developed in this paper can be extended to richer term structure models or discount factor specifications.

Code Availability:The replication code is available in the Harvard Dataverse athttps://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/MDDVGR.

Footnotes

1 The year 1975 marks the end of restrictions on U.S. private gold investments in place since 1933. The official U.S. gold peg was ended in 1971.

2 Data are from Bloomberg, the Federal Reserve Bank of St Louis, and the Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia. The sample length is limited by the availability of inflation forecasts.

3 Metal prices are from Bloomberg based on London market prices, except for Platinum, where the Bloomberg currency quote XPT is used because of its longer sample. For monthly and annual results the difference between London market prices and the currency quotes is immaterial.

4 Bloomberg currency quotes XAU, XAG, and XPT are used for better intraday time alignment. Daily regressions with London fixing prices have very low R -squared.

5 The requirement that the elements of M are positive limits admissible values for Δ. In the more general case studied below the elements of M are specified through the exponential function to guarantee positivity.

6 The deterministic growth trend γ can be introduced by replacing β by β γ ⁠ , and M ~ by γ M ~ ⁠ .

7 Default-free real bonds are assumed to have geometric amortization, and their price is determined by p t λ = E t M t , t + 1 [ c + λ + ( 1 − λ ) p t + 1 λ ] with ( 1 / λ ) the bond’s (average) maturity, and c the coupon rate. Iterating forward the price equation, the gross yield to maturity satisfies Y t ( λ ) = ( c + λ ) / p t λ − λ + 1 ⁠ .

8 According to the World Gold Council (2024) , the United States has accounted for 6.5% of the world total jewelry demand on average between 2010 and 2023, with jewelry accounting for 50% of the total demand.

9 Iterating forward on the pricing equation of the RA model for a constant v implies p t / v = ∑ j = 0 ∞ γ j E t M t , t + 1 + j , illustrating the role of the only free parameter γ .
